learning_outcomes.description: Identify the ways in which popular culture in Australia contributes to the construction of national, cultural and individual identity.
Understand and apply a range of approaches used in the study of popular culture.
Situate popular culture in Australia within a global context
Collect, analyse and organise information about specific fields of popular culture
Critically engage with contemporary academic writing and media.
Critically reflect on your own engagement with popular culture.

assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Reflection, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
Each week you will be expected to actively engage with a range of undertakings designed to expand and assess your understanding of that week's topic. You will also be required to complete a written reflection based on the lecture, required learning resources, and in-class activities. You will need to attend the lecture and read/watch/listen to the required learning material as well as join in the class activities to complete this assessment.

The written reflection is a device and note free exercise conducted under exam conditions. You will be required to close and remove all devices and notes from your table. You will be supplied with paper, but you will need to bring your own pen.

The tutorial learning activities and written reflection are a valuable learning activity, providing you with the opportunity to foster your skills in critical thinking, language development, communication, teamwork, written literacy, observation and interpretation of media texts, and visual literacy.

There will be 10 tutorial activities across the semester, each worth 5%

Only your best 7 results count, together forming 35% of your final grade.

Further details are provided on Blackboard, including specific instructions for each topic.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission is not possible for this piece of assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
4/09/2026 4:00 pm

This due date is for your rationale. Your physical poster will be submitted in tutorials in week 6, further details will be provided on Blackboard
assessments.mode: Activity/ Performance, Product/ Artefact/ Multimedia, Written
assessments.task_description:
This assessment is designed to give you the opportunity to expand your knowledge and understanding of an aspect of Australian popular culture from the course that is of interest to you. The assessment has a creative and a scholarly element. You will develop skills in abstract thinking, problem solving, research, critical thinking, and the ability to communicate complex ideas visually and in written form. 

You will choose ONE reading from the Further Readings List provided on Blackboard. The readings listed here expand on the course topics, but they do not necessarily fit neatly with one topic. Most traverse topics especially in relation to gender and First Nations perspectives. You may choose any reading that interests you. The idea is that you will explore an aspect of Australian popular culture that goes beyond what you have learned from the required resources. 

You will make a physical poster as a creative response to your chosen Further Reading. This will be submitted in tutorials (details provided on Blackboard).

You will submit a rationale for your physical poster that also includes a scanned/photographed digital image of your poster. Your rationale must actively and meaningfully engage with a minimum of 3 scholarly peer-reviewed readings (plus other relevant non-scholarly material). One of the readings will be your chosen reading from the Further Readings List. The other scholarly readings must be from the course material. Your rationale has a twofold function: Firstly, is will establish your understanding of the further reading and of the topic(s) as a whole. Secondly, it will introduce your poster.

Full details of what to include in your rationale along with full details about how to approach this assessment as a whole are provided on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This assessment provides you with the opportunity to develop your written communication skills by applying complex theory, research-based evidence and analysis to case studies. You will further develop your proficiency in research skills, abstract thinking, organisation, and working to a deadline.

For this assessment you are required to compile a compendium on an aspect of Australian popular culture covered in the course. You must choose a topic that focuses on a DIFFERENT aspect of Australian popular culture to your Physical Poster and Rationale. 

A full and detailed list of topic options will be provided on Blackboard, along with detailed guidelines, criteria and sample compendiums. The compendium must actively engage with at least 6 relevant peer-reviewed scholarly sources from the course, plus a range of relevant media sources. Total length is 2000 words +/- 10% (reference lists, quotes, and figure notations not included).

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1 Lecture: Introducing Australian Popular Culture

This important first lecture will provide an orientation to the course. It will outline what we will study and how and explain the assessment tasks. We will also begin to define key concepts and explore central themes.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1 Lecture: Introducing Australian Popular Culture This important first lecture will provide an orientation to the course. It will outline what we will study and how and explain the assessment tasks. We will also begin to define key concepts and explore central themes.

There are no tutorials this week. Tutorials begin in Week 2.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2 Lecture: Defining and Analysing Popular Culture

In this course we approach the study of popular culture though the field of cultural studies. Cultural studies is a complex, dynamic field of intellectual activity that borrows concepts, ideas and methodologies from a range of humanities disciplines - especially history, sociology, philosophy, literary studies, anthropology, political theory - in the attempt to make sense of the social world around us.

What is culture? What is popular culture? Is it simply culture that is popular? How popular does something have to be to be considered popular culture? Is the distinction between high and popular culture still legitimate? Has it ever been? These are some of the questions that we will consider this week as we define our key field of study.

What is culture? What is popular culture? Is it simply culture that is popular? How popular does something have to be to be considered popular culture? Is the distinction between high and popular culture still legitimate? Has it ever been? These are some of the questions that we will consider this week as we define our key field of study.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3 Lecture: Gender Representations in Popular Culture

Popular culture is a key site where gender and gender identities are produced, represented and consumed. This week we are concerned with how men and women are (and have been) represented in Australian popular culture. We will examine some dominant, reoccurring gender tropes that emerge in Australian popular culture, the origins and ideological underpinnings of these tropes and archetypes, as well as some of the ways these tropes are reconfigured and destabilised.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4 Lecture: Indigenous Representations in Popular Culture

In this lecture we will examine some of the historical, social, and cultural contexts that have produced Aboriginality in Australia. We will explore some of the ways that popular culture has represented Aboriginality and the ways that First Nations peoples have looked to mainstream popular culture to voice their own concerns and express their own identities.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5 Lecture: Australian Cinema

Australia has a long film history, indeed, the first feature film in the world was made in Australia - The Story of the Kelly Gang (1906). Melbourne's Limelight Film Studio was also one of the world's first production houses. The lecture this week will provide an overview of the history of Australian cinema and to examine Aussie cinema in the context of the films themselves, industry and policy considerations, and audience consumption and reception.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6: Lecture: Sports and Nation

In Australia, sport is often said to be a national obsession and this love of sport is invoked as central to Australian identity. This week we will examine how and why sport has become a central vehicle for defining national identity and examine sports in the context of race and gender.

This week we will examine how and why sport has become a central vehicle for defining national identity and examine sports in the context of race and gender.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7 Lecture: Australian Comedy and Humour

The desire to find humour in situations is a fundamental human characteristic. But while the pleasure of laughter is ubiquitous, comedy is also complex, localised and embedded in deep cultural meanings, histories, and contexts. This weeks lecture will establish a theoretical framework with which to analyse Australian comedy and humour.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8 Lecture: Australian Music Cultures

Music is everywhere. Often it is just in the background, in the places we spend time during the day, like the supermarket or our local cafe. Perhaps we pay it barely any attention. At other times we consciously choose to listen to a particular performer, to go to a gig or a festival. Music can be a form of entertainment, a source of comfort, a physical and mental escape. It can have powerful physical and emotional effects. Our musical preferences and tastes can provide a window into our attitudes and values. Music can play a significant role in shaping our identities.But does Australian music have a distinctive sound, style, or history? This will be a starting point for the topic this week.

It can have powerful physical and emotional effects. Our musical preferences and tastes can provide a window into our attitudes and values. Music can play a significant role in shaping our identities.But does Australian music have a distinctive sound, style, or history? This will be a starting point for the topic this week.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9 Lecture: Australian Car Cultures

The car is a technology that has significantly reconfigured society in terms of how people live their lives. This week will be turbo charged by two important aspects of Australian car culture: Firstly, the car as a symbol of freedom, offering the opportunity to explore new, remote places and the way this manifests in popular culture, particularly in the road movie (think WolfCreek, Last Cab to Darwin, Doing Time for Patsy Cline, Priscilla, Queen of the Desert...just to name a few examples). Secondly, cars in suburbia - again looking at how this manifests in popular culture.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11 Lecture: Surf and Beach Cultures

What is surf culture and how is it defined? What can be gained by reading Australian surfing practices as a cultural text? How does the beach, as a natural phenomenon, become a cultural site? These questions are central to this weeks focus on surfing and beach cultures in Australia.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 11 Lecture: Surf and Beach Cultures What is surf culture and how is it defined? What can be gained by reading Australian surfing practices as a cultural text? How does the beach, as a natural phenomenon, become a cultural site? These questions are central to this weeks focus on surfing and beach cultures in Australia.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12 Lecture: Food and Cafe Cultures

Chicken Parmi's, Twisties, Tim Tams, fairy bread, Cherry Ripes, Iced VoVos, and Vegemite are just some of the many 'food' products hailed as quintessentially Australian. But since the 1950s, Australian eating habits, both inside and outside the home, have changed dramatically. Today, Australian cuisine is celebrated for its quality and diversity. This week we will explore how food cultures in Australia have evolved.
